hypothesis: Narrow scope (constriction) increases throughput (L+P), with diminishing returns at extreme constriction (stall analog)
method: Joined SESSION-LOG L+P (n=192) with git-log file/domain scope (n=181). Scope metrics: n_domains (distinct domains/ dirs), n_files, n_dirs (2-level dir paths). Bernoulli test: bin by domain count, compute mean L+P. Controlled for era (pre-domain <S180, early-domain S180-S329, DOMEX-era S330-S369, mature S370+) and session type (DOMEX vs non-DOMEX). Inverse scope (1/n_dirs) tested as constriction proxy.

results.era_controlled_correlation: r=+0.117 (DOMEX-era+, n=37) — near zero after era control

f_evo1_comparison.explanation: Original was confounded by session size (L-300 noted this). scope_diversity_ratio != absolute scope. Large-n retest shows no focus advantage.
f_evo1_comparison.L-300_own_recommendation: Measure absolute unique-scope count, re-test with 10+ sessions

verdict: FALSIFIED — constriction does NOT increase throughput. Broader scope correlates with MORE L+P (r=+0.354). After era control, correlation near-zero (r=+0.117). Session TYPE (DOMEX) and ERA explain most variance, not scope. Bernoulli pipe analogy is wrong: swarm throughput is not a conserved quantity that accelerates through constriction. F-EVO1 r=-0.835 non-replication at 20x sample size.
implications: F-EVO1 focus prescription should be retired or weakened
Scope is a confound with session type, not a causal driver
Bernoulli fluid dynamics analogy does not transfer to knowledge production
Turbulent (broad) = productive, consistent with F-FLD1 finding
